summary: The author achieved 37th place in a biomass regression competition by leveraging a frozen DINOv3 backbone with a novel Visual Prompt Tuning architecture. Key innovations included appending learnable task and prompt tokens, initializing them via self-prompt tuning with Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization, and enforcing token diversity with an orthogonality loss. The solution heavily relied on severe information bottlenecking through multi-crop training and random cropping, which allowed controlled overfitting without harming generalization. This approach, combined with state-stratified GroupKFold validation and full-dataset training without early stopping, yielded strong leaderboard alignment and robust predictions.
extensive_summary:
## Backbone Selection and Rationale
The author evaluated DINOv2, DINOv3, and EVA02, ultimately selecting DINOv3 due to its superior generalization. Despite EVA02 showing higher cross-validation scores, DINOv3 exhibited a smaller train-validation loss gap. The author attributes this to DINOv3's self-supervised training objective and its retention of rich second-order statistics via Gram matrix information, which proved crucial for capturing subtle spatial cues in dense prediction tasks.

## Model Architecture and Token Management
The final architecture combines Visual Prompt Tuning with DETR-style query tokens (task tokens). Four learnable task tokens (one per target) and sixteen prompt tokens are appended to the input. During the forward pass, the backbone processes all tokens jointly. Predictions are generated by concatenating task token embeddings with global average pooled (GAP) and standard deviation pooled (STD) features from patch tokens, passed through target-specific heads. To prevent token collapse, an explicit orthogonality loss was introduced to keep tokens decorrelated.

## Self Prompt Tuning Initialization
Task and prompt tokens were initialized using a self-prompt tuning strategy. For each target, the ten training samples with the highest values were selected, their CLS token embeddings were averaged, and this mean representation initialized the corresponding task token. Prompt tokens were grouped by target and initialized similarly. To counteract the resulting correlated initialization, Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization was applied at initialization time to ensure mutual orthogonality before training.

## Training Strategy and Information Bottlenecking
The author discovered that cross-validation strategy alignment was critical, settling on state-stratified GroupKFold splits over sampling dates. A major insight emerged from training on the full dataset without validation until the mean cross-validation training loss was reached. The most impactful technique was severe information bottlenecking: instead of using full images, the model was trained on random 448x448 crops (covering ~10% of the 1000x2000 image). Multi-crop training (mean aggregation of predictions) allowed controlled overfitting; training R² could rise to 0.95 while validation R² plateaued at 0.72, yet leaderboard scores continued to improve incrementally. This demonstrated that the model learned stable generalizable features despite memorizing noise.

## Inference and Ensembling
During inference, images were tiled into 500x500 patches, with a central 448x448 crop extracted. Test-time augmentation used simple geometric flips. The final submission ensembled multiple checkpoints from the same training run. EMA and SWA were tested but did not significantly improve performance.
competition_flow: Raw 1000x2000 images are split into 500x500 tiles with central 448x448 crops, processed through a frozen DINOv3 backbone with learnable task/prompt tokens, and aggregated via target-specific heads to predict biomass targets, with ensembling applied across multiple checkpoints.
data_processing: Multi-crop training with mean aggregation of predictions
Test-time augmentation via horizontal, vertical, and combined geometric flips
cv_strategy: State-stratified GroupKFold splits over sampling dates
ensembling: Ensembled multiple checkpoints from the same training run; tested EMA and SWA but found they did not significantly improve performance.
insights: Limited dataset size penalized overfitting and forced confrontation of true generalization ability.
Cross-validation strategy alignment with leaderboard performance is critical and should be prioritized early.
Severe information bottlenecking via multi-crop training allows controlled overfitting without harming generalization.
Training on the full dataset without validation until mean CV training loss is reached can outperform ensembling CV models.
DINOv3's self-supervised objective and Gram matrix retention make it superior for dense prediction tasks requiring subtle spatial cues.
critical_findings: Allowing training R² to rise to 0.95 while validation R² plateaued at 0.72 still yielded incremental leaderboard gains, proving the model wasn't unlearning generalizable features.
Switching from two-stream concatenation/summation to mean aggregation of half-image predictions dramatically improved robustness by forcing each stream to predict the full region's biomass.
EVA02 achieved higher cross-validation scores but consistently underperformed DINOv3 on the leaderboard due to poorer generalization.
what_did_not_work: Two-stream concatenation of embeddings increased model complexity and sensitivity to noise.
Two-stream summation suffered from lack of supervision on which half contributed more to the error.
Using pseudo-labels from a strong ensemble to resolve two-stream summation limitations did not yield improvements.
Exponential moving average and stochastic weight averaging did not meaningfully raise the performance ceiling.
